section: Chapter 4: Living organisms characteristics and habitats

questions.question: What is the main difference between living and non-living things?
questions.correct_answer: Living things have characteristics such as cellular organization, metabolism, growth and development, reproduction, response to stimuli, homeostasis, and adaptation.
questions.explanation: These characteristics distinguish living organisms from non-living things.

questions.question: What is the role of cheetah's spotted coat?
questions.correct_answer: Camouflage in the tall grasses of the savanna
questions.explanation: Their spotted coat provides camouflage in the tall grasses of the savanna.

questions.question: How do mangroves adapt to high levels of salt in their environment?
questions.correct_answer: They have specialized salt-excreting glands or filtering systems in their roots
questions.explanation: Mangroves have specialized salt-excreting glands or filtering systems in their roots to deal with high levels of salt in the soil or water.

questions.question: What adaptation helps camels survive long periods without water?
questions.explanation: Camels have water-storing humps that allow them to survive long periods without water.

questions.question: What is the function of a lion's mane?
questions.correct_answer: It helps make male lions appear larger and more intimidating to rivals, signalling their dominance in the wild
questions.explanation: A lion’s mane is a powerful symbol of strength, dominance, and maturity.

questions.question: How do wolf packs operate?
questions.correct_answer: Wolf packs often operate more cooperatively, with both male and female parents guiding the pack
questions.explanation: Recent research has shown that wolf packs in the wild often operate more cooperatively.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of a cheetah?
questions.explanation: Cheetahs are the fastest land animals, with long, muscular legs and a streamlined body to help them reach incredible speeds while hunting.

questions.question: What is the main adaptation of mangroves?
questions.correct_answer: They have specialized salt-excreting glands or filtering systems in their roots
questions.explanation: Mangroves have specialized salt-excreting glands or filtering systems in their roots to deal with high levels of salt in the soil or water.

questions.question: What is unique about a lion's mane?
questions.correct_answer: It can grow up to 16 centimetres (6.2 inches) long
questions.explanation: A lion’s mane can grow up to 16 centimetres (6.2 inches) long.

questions.question: What is the main function of a wolf's howl?
questions.correct_answer: It signals the presence of an alpha
questions.explanation: A grey wolf’s howl echoes across 25 square kilometers, signaling the presence of an alpha.

notes.title: Characteristics of Living Organisms
notes.content: Living things have characteristics such as cellular organization, metabolism, growth and development, reproduction, response to stimuli, homeostasis, and adaptation.

notes.title: Cheetah Adaptation
notes.content: Their long, muscular legs and a streamlined body help them reach incredible speeds while hunting.

notes.title: Mangrove Adaptation
notes.content: They have specialized salt-excreting glands or filtering systems in their roots to deal with high levels of salt in the soil or water.

notes.title: Camel Adaptation
notes.content: Their water-storing humps allow them to survive long periods without water.

notes.title: Lion's Mane
notes.content: It helps make male lions appear larger and more intimidating to rivals, signalling their dominance in the wild.

notes.title: Wolf Pack Operation
notes.content: Wolf packs often operate more cooperatively, with both male and female parents guiding the pack.

facts.fact: Cheetahs are the fastest land animals, reaching speeds of up to 120 km/h
Mangroves have specialized salt-excreting glands or filtering systems in their roots
Camels can survive long periods without water due to their water-storing humps
A lion’s mane can grow up to 16 centimetres (6.2 inches) long
Wolf packs often operate more cooperatively, with both male and female parents guiding the pack
